value: Headline: Crude got slammed despite a bullish EIA print — WTI settled $70.09, down $3.12, and Brent fell $3.71 to $73.37 as the geopolitical risk premium drained out of the barrel with Hormuz shipping normalizing. The week-ending June 19 WPSR showed a 6.1 mb crude draw to 412.1 mb and Cushing down another 1.1 mb to 18.96 mb — firmly below the operational floor — but easing Middle East tensions and a softening demand narrative overwhelmed the constructive inventory story. The tape is screaming risk-off supply relief, not fundamentals. Crude & products: Per the latest weekly EIA report (week ending June 19, 2026), commercial crude drew 6.1 mb to 412.1 mb on high run rates and steady export pull, with 4-week crude exports at 4.93 mb/d against 5.75 mb/d imports. Cushing fell 1.1 mb to 18.96 mb — already below the ~25 mb tank-bottom minimum, where deliverability stress and basis volatility become structural risks rather than tail events. Products diverged from crude: gasoline built 2.1 mb to 216.3 mb and distillate built 3.0 mb to 106.1 mb, with jet up 1.3 mb to 46.3 mb. Refinery utilization eased 0.6 pts to 96.1% — still elevated heading into peak summer, keeping crude inputs at 17.11 mb/d and the demand pull on barrels intense. Refining margins: Despite the flat-price rout, cracks held firm — the 3:2:1 sits at $51.29, with the distillate crack a robust $60.21 and gasoline at $46.82. The product builds capped the upside, but the collapse in crude relative to products is mechanically supportive for margins: RBOB fell only 8 cents to $2.7832/gal and ULSD barely budged, off a penny to $3.1019/gal, while crude bled $3+. That divergence is the refiner's friend here — falling feedstock against sticky product prices widens the box, and the strong distillate crack signals diesel tightness persists even as stocks build. Curve structure: WTI holds backwardation with M1-M2 at $0.38 and M1-M12 at $3.34, confirming prompt tightness even as flat price cracks lower — consistent with sub-floor Cushing. Brent's M1-M2 has slipped into a marginal $0.24 contango while M1-M12 backwardation flattened to $1.93, a sign the front-month risk premium is unwinding faster than the deferred curve. The Brent-WTI spread sits at just $3.30 — narrow, which weakens the US export arb and threatens to keep barrels home; with exports already running 4.93 mb/d, a sustained sub-$4 spread points to softer outbound flows and potential domestic crude builds ahead. To re-open the arb, WTI needs to fall relative to Brent. News & geopolitics: The dominant driver is de-escalation: WTI slid to its lowest since March as Hormuz shipping gradually normalizes, with prices hitting a four-month low as easing tensions boost the supply outlook. JP Morgan cut its year-end Brent forecast to $78, citing a demand miss and inventory lag — a bearish institutional anchor reinforced by China's crude imports plunging to their lowest since 2018, a demand red flag for the world's top importer. Supply-side, Norway's output again beat forecasts despite the pullback from April highs, adding non-OPEC barrels, while Russia's refining woes deepen — Rosneft is pushing firms to refine 30% of crude domestically and a Moscow refinery may stay offline until 2027, tightening Russian product balances even as crude flows. Rubio's Gulf trip amid Iran-deal anxiety keeps a geopolitical floor under the tape, but for now the market is pricing relief, not risk. Lower crude is already rippling into equities — United Airlines shares jumped on the fuel tailwind, and the euro sank to a one-year low as the oil drop fuels ECB rate-cut bets.
